fr.elements: Main Building; Detached Structure; Front Driveway; Landscaped Area; Roof System; Open Green Space; Perimeter Fencing
fr.considerations: Single Access Point; Multiple Structures; Dense Vegetation
fr.recommendations: Ensuring the single access point remains clear and unobstructed could enhance responder access and facilitate timely entry to the property. Considering multiple structures on the property, clearly marking pathways and entry points to each structure may assist in efficient navigation and rapid response. Managing dense vegetation, particularly near the driveway and around structures, may improve visibility for responders and aid in safe maneuvering and equipment deployment. Keeping open green spaces unobstructed could support staging activities and movement of emergency vehicles. Clearly distinguishing the perimeter fencing may help responders quickly identify property boundaries and adapt their approach accordingly during emergency operations.

wildfire.conditions: Scattered Trees; Scattered Shrubs; Vegetated Perimeter; Open Terrain; Limited Defensible Space; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Multiple Access Routes
wildfire.recommendations: Enhancing the separation between the scattered trees and shrubs and the structure could improve defensible space and reduce potential fire spread to the building. Managing the vegetated perimeter through regular pruning and clearing of underbrush may reduce fuel continuity and enhance the effectiveness of existing access routes. Attention should be given to maintaining a clear zone around the structure by reducing vegetation directly adjacent to it, limiting opportunities for fire exposure. With multiple access routes available, ensuring these pathways are kept clear of overhanging branches and dense vegetation will further support firefighter access and suppression efforts. Preserving the open terrain and expanding upon it where feasible can create additional natural firebreaks that contribute to the long-term resilience and preparedness of the property. Regularly assessing and adapting vegetation management practices can help sustain these efforts over time, ultimately supporting the property's capacity to withstand wildfire events.
